title: Quiz: Quantum Monte Carlo Methods
description: Test your understanding of quantum Monte Carlo algorithms and their speedup

questions.question: What is the sample complexity of classical Monte Carlo to achieve error epsilon in estimating an expectation?
questions.explanation: Classical Monte Carlo has standard error sigma/sqrt(N), so achieving error epsilon requires N = O(sigma^2/epsilon^2) = O(1/epsilon^2) samples.

questions.question: In quantum mean estimation, how is the expectation E[f(X)] encoded in the quantum state?

options.text: As a phase of the quantum state
As the probability of measuring an ancilla qubit in |1>
As the number of qubits needed

questions.explanation: The state is prepared such that the amplitude of the ancilla being in |1> is sqrt(E[f(X)]), so the probability of measuring |1> equals E[f(X)].

questions.question: What is amplitude encoding in the context of quantum Monte Carlo?

options.text: Encoding data in the phase of qubits
Creating a state |psi> = sum sqrt(p_i)|i> to encode distribution p
Using amplitude amplification to boost success probability
Measuring amplitudes directly on quantum hardware

questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding creates a quantum state where the amplitude of each basis state |i> is sqrt(p_i), encoding the probability distribution in the state amplitudes.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a source of error in quantum Monte Carlo?
questions.explanation: The central limit theorem is a mathematical result that helps analyze classical Monte Carlo; it's not a source of error. Discretization, amplitude estimation precision, and quantum noise are actual error sources.

questions.question: Quantum Monte Carlo can provide speedup for any function, regardless of how the function is computed.
questions.explanation: False. The function must be efficiently computable by a quantum circuit. If the function evaluation itself requires exponential resources, the quantum speedup for sampling doesn't help.

questions.question: For quantum integration of f(x) over [0,1], how is the integral related to quantum mean estimation?

options.text: The integral equals E[f(U)] where U is uniform on [0,1]
The integral is the phase of the final state
The integral equals the fidelity with a target state
The integral is computed by quantum Fourier transform

questions.explanation: The integral of f(x) over [0,1] equals the expectation E[f(U)] where U is uniformly distributed. Quantum mean estimation then estimates this expectation with quadratic speedup.

questions.question: What is the main practical challenge for near-term quantum Monte Carlo implementations?
questions.explanation: Quantum Monte Carlo with amplitude estimation requires deep circuits (O(1/epsilon) Grover iterations), which exceeds current hardware coherence times. This is the main near-term challenge.

questions.question: Which applications can benefit from quantum Monte Carlo speedup? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Option pricing, risk analysis, and high-dimensional integration all involve computing expectations that benefit from QMC speedup. Sorting is not a sampling problem and doesn't benefit from QMC.
